notebook_title: Self-Attention from Scratch: Query-Key-Value, the Matrix View, Multi-Head, and Positional Encoding
learning_objectives: Explain why self-attention exists: a fully-connected/windowed layer only sees a fixed neighborhood, while self-attention lets every position in a variable-length sequence attend to every other position.
Implement scaled dot-product attention from scratch for a single output vector: project each input into query/key/value, score q against every k, softmax the scores, and take a value-weighted sum.
Re-express the same computation in matrix form (Q=W^q I, K=W^k I, V=W^v I, A=K^T Q, A'=softmax(A), O=V A') and verify the vectorized result numerically matches the per-element result.
Visualize the attention matrix A' as a heatmap and see the effect of the 1/sqrt(d_k) scaling factor.
Extend to multi-head self-attention, computing attention independently per head, concatenating, and projecting by W^O, and compare the per-head attention patterns.
Show that self-attention is permutation-equivariant and inject order information with sinusoidal positional encoding, visualizing the encoding and its effect.

cells.goal: Title cell and big-picture framing: introduce self-attention as the core building block of Transformers. Motivate it by contrasting a fully-connected/windowed layer (sees only a fixed neighborhood) with self-attention (every position attends to every other position in a variable-length sequence). State that the notebook builds the mechanism from scratch in NumPy and then visualizes it. List the learning objectives.
cells.widget_plan: None (narrative markdown with motivation diagram description and an objectives bullet list).

cells.goal: Environment setup: import numpy, matplotlib, and ipywidgets. Define a set_seed() helper for reproducibility and a numerically-stable softmax(x, axis) helper that is reused throughout the notebook. Print confirmation that imports succeeded so the notebook is self-contained on a free Colab CPU runtime.
cells.widget_plan: None (setup cell). Keep imports minimal (numpy, matplotlib, ipywidgets) so it runs without installs on Colab.

cells.goal: Build the shared toy problem: define a small synthetic input sequence I (e.g. a 4x4 or 4x6 matrix of input vectors a^1..a^N, optionally tied to short token labels like ['the','cat','sat','down']) and the learned projection matrices W^q, W^k, W^v with a chosen model dimension d and key dimension d_k. Fix the seed so every later cell shares the same inputs and weights. This is the single source of inputs/weights for the per-element, matrix, multi-head, and positional-encoding demos.
outputs: I (input matrix, shape [N, d] or [d, N] with a documented convention)
cells.widget_plan: None (shared data + parameter definition). Small fixed dimensions keep all visualizations legible.

cells.goal: Section header and explanation for scaled dot-product (Query-Key-Value) attention: define q=W^q a, k=W^k a, v=W^v a; the attention score alpha_{i,j}=q^i . k^j scaled by 1/sqrt(d_k); softmax over j to get alpha'_{i,j}; and the output b^i = sum_j alpha'_{i,j} v^j. Note dot-product vs additive scoring. Preview that the next cell computes b^1 step by step.
cells.widget_plan: None (conceptual markdown with the QKV equations).

cells.goal: Compute one output vector b^1 from scratch, step by step, to make the mechanism concrete: project all inputs to q/k/v, take query q^1, compute raw scores q^1 . k^j for every j, scale by 1/sqrt(d_k), apply softmax to get alpha'_{1,j}, and form b^1 = sum_j alpha'_{1,j} v^j. Print each intermediate (q^1, all k^j, raw scores, scaled scores, softmax weights, final b^1) so students can trace every number.
inputs: I, W_q, W_k, W_v, d_k from C03
outputs: Q, K, V matrices for the toy sequence
Printed q^1, scores, alpha'_{1,j}, and b^1
cells.widget_plan: None (transparent step-by-step computation with printed intermediates).

cells.goal: Interactive single-position attention demo: a slider/dropdown to choose the query position i (1..N). For the chosen i, compute the attention weights alpha'_{i,j} over all keys and the output b^i, then display a bar chart of the weights (labeled by token) so students see which positions position i attends to and how the weighted sum is formed. Render a default position so Run-all produces output.
outputs: Bar chart of attention weights for the chosen query position
cells.widget_plan: ipywidgets: IntSlider or Dropdown (query position i, 1..N) plus an Output area; matplotlib bar chart of alpha'_{i,j} over tokens. A default invocation runs once for a fixed i so Run-all yields output before interaction.

cells.goal: Section header and explanation for the matrix formulation: show that stacking inputs into I lets all q/k/v be produced by single matrix multiplies (Q=W^q I, K=W^k I, V=W^v I), the full attention matrix is A=K^T Q (scaled), A'=softmax(A) column-wise, and all outputs at once are O=V A'. Emphasize that only W^q, W^k, W^v are learned and the computation is fully parallel.
cells.widget_plan: None (conceptual markdown with the matrix equations and a note on parallelism).

cells.goal: Vectorized self-attention in one pass: compute Q, K, V by matrix multiply, form the scaled attention matrix A = K^T Q / sqrt(d_k), apply softmax to get A', and compute O = V A' for ALL output vectors at once. Numerically assert that column 1 of O equals the b^1 computed step-by-step in C05 (and that the chosen b^i from C06 matches), proving the per-element and matrix views are identical.
inputs: I, W_q, W_k, W_v, d_k from C03
outputs: Assertion / printed check that O matches the per-element result
cells.widget_plan: None (vectorized computation with a correctness assertion against C05).

cells.goal: Interactive attention-matrix heatmap: render A' as a heatmap with token labels on both axes so students see the full all-pairs attention pattern. Provide a toggle for whether to apply the 1/sqrt(d_k) scaling and a slider for a softmax temperature so the effect of scaling on the sharpness of attention is visible. Render a default (scaled) heatmap for Run-all.
inputs: A (pre-softmax scores) and Q, K, d_k from C08
outputs: Heatmap of the attention matrix A'
Visible difference between scaled and unscaled attention
cells.widget_plan: ipywidgets: ToggleButtons/Checkbox (apply 1/sqrt(d_k) scaling) and a FloatSlider (temperature); matplotlib imshow heatmap with token tick labels and a colorbar. Renders a default heatmap once for Run-all.

cells.goal: Section header and explanation for multi-head self-attention: explain that each q/k/v is split into multiple heads (q^{i,1}, q^{i,2}, ...), each head computes attention independently to capture 'different types of relevance', and the per-head outputs are concatenated and projected by W^O to form b^i. Preview the side-by-side per-head attention maps in the next cell.
cells.widget_plan: None (conceptual markdown with the multi-head equations).

cells.goal: Implement 2-head (parameterizable) self-attention: split Q/K/V into per-head subspaces, compute an attention matrix and output per head, concatenate the head outputs, and project by a W^O to produce the final outputs. Display the per-head attention heatmaps side by side so students can compare how different heads attend to different positions. Verify output shape matches single-head O.
cells.widget_plan: ipywidgets (optional): IntSlider for number of heads (divisors of d) with a default 2-head run; matplotlib subplot grid of per-head heatmaps. Renders a default 2-head result for Run-all.

cells.goal: Section header and explanation for positional encoding: point out that self-attention is permutation-equivariant (it has no built-in notion of order), so a unique positional vector e^i must be added to each input a^i. Introduce hand-crafted sinusoidal encodings (and mention learned/relative alternatives) and preview the heatmap and permutation demo.
cells.widget_plan: None (conceptual markdown explaining permutation-equivariance and positional encoding).

cells.goal: Build and visualize sinusoidal positional encoding: implement positional_encoding(seq_len, d) producing the classic sin/cos table, render it as a heatmap, and demonstrate permutation-equivariance — show that permuting the input sequence permutes the attention output identically WITHOUT positional encoding, but adding e^i to each input breaks that symmetry so order now matters. An interactive control lets students change sequence length / toggle encoding on and off.
inputs: I, W_q, W_k, W_v from C03
outputs: Heatmap of the sinusoidal positional encoding table
Demonstration that attention is permutation-equivariant without PE and order-aware with PE
cells.widget_plan: ipywidgets: IntSlider (sequence length) and a Checkbox/ToggleButtons (add positional encoding on/off); matplotlib heatmap of the encoding plus a printed before/after permutation comparison. Renders a default encoding heatmap for Run-all.

cells.goal: Wrap-up: summarize the journey from per-element QKV attention to the parallel matrix formulation, multi-head attention, and positional encoding. Restate key takeaways (attention is a softmax-weighted average of values; only W^q/W^k/W^v are learned; the matrix view is fully parallel; heads capture different relevances; positions must be injected). Note that this toy NumPy implementation is exactly the primitive used inside real Transformers (BERT, ViT, GPT) and point to further reading.
cells.widget_plan: None (summary markdown with references to the source lecture and Transformer applications).

global_flow_notes: The notebook follows a build-from-scratch then generalize-and-visualize structure. C01-C03 frame the problem and establish a single shared toy sequence I plus learned projection matrices W^q/W^k/W^v with reusable set_seed() and softmax() helpers, so every later cell operates on the same numbers. The four concepts then build strictly on each other: per-element QKV attention (C04-C06) computes one output b^1 transparently and lets students inspect attention weights for any query position; the matrix formulation (C07-C09) recomputes ALL outputs at once and asserts numerical equivalence with the per-element result, then visualizes the attention matrix and the role of 1/sqrt(d_k) scaling; multi-head attention (C10-C11) splits the shared Q/K/V into heads and compares per-head attention maps; positional encoding (C12-C13) demonstrates permutation-equivariance and injects sinusoidal positions. Critical execution chain: C02 -> C03 -> C05 -> C06, and C05 -> C08 -> {C09, C11, C13}. Every interactive cell renders a default (non-interactive) result so a fresh Run-all used by Stage 5 verification produces valid output before any widget is touched. C14 synthesizes all demos. Interactive controls (query position, scaling/temperature, head count, sequence length / PE on-off) are each chosen to make a specific pedagogical contrast visible rather than for decoration.
assumptions: The demo uses NumPy and matplotlib only (with ipywidgets for interactivity) on small synthetic sequences, so every cell runs in seconds on a free Colab CPU runtime with no model download, dataset, or API key.

A consistent matrix-shape convention (documented in C03) is used throughout; the lecture's K^T Q form is followed, and helpers are written so the per-element (C05) and matrix (C08) computations are asserted to match.
ipywidgets and matplotlib render in Google Colab; interactive cells degrade gracefully (a default invocation plus widget controls) so they remain valid under a non-interactive Run-all.
Self-attention-vs-CNN/RNN and the applications survey from the lecture are out of scope as executable demos and appear only as brief framing/wrap-up context, per the Stage 1 selection rationale.

Target audience knows basic linear algebra (vectors, matrices, dot product, matrix multiplication, transpose) and softmax; the notebook reinforces these via printed intermediates rather than re-teaching them.
Target runtime is Google Colab (Python 3.12, numpy 2.0.2) per the Stage 5 verification environment.
